did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with a Tabular model, specifically employing the DenseNet 201 architecture and Transfer Learning via the FastAI library, to classify melanoma skin lesions from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a novel Convolutional Neural Network model integrated with a Tabular model for melanoma classification. It details specific methodology (DenseNet 201, Transfer Learning, FastAI), dataset usage (SIIM-ISIC 2019, HAM10000, BCN_20000), implementation steps, and reports new empirical results (95.8% accuracy, 91.6% ROCAUC), distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Melanoma Classification using Convolutional Neural Network Model Integrated with Tabular Model
journal: International Journal of Advance Research, Ideas and Innovations in Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma classification by proposing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with a Tabular model to improve diagnostic accuracy. The researchers utilized the SIIM-ISIC 2019 dataset, which combines the HAM10000 and BCN_20000 datasets, to train and evaluate their model. The methodology employed the DenseNet 201 architecture with transfer learning via the FastAI library and a custom PyTorch-based 'image_tabular' library for data integration. To handle class imbalance and data redundancy, the team implemented duplicate removal, custom loss function weights, and differential learning rates. The model achieved a final accuracy of 95.8% and an ROC AUC of 91.6%, demonstrating that integrating tabular data with dermoscopy images enhances melanoma detection performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification task. The paper states the dataset is highly imbalanced with approximately 2% of images being malignant (melanoma) and 98% benign. The model aims to classify malignant and benign melanoma cells.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The paper notes that approximately 98% of the images in the SIIM-ISIC 2019 dataset (comprising HAM10000 and BCN_20000) are benign lesions. Specific benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, BCC) are not explicitly listed as separate output classes in the experiment description, but are implied by the dataset composition.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) or benign categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify malignant and benign melanoma cells' and 'predicting whether the skin lesions are malignant or not.' The dataset is described as highly imbalanced with approximately 2% malignant and 98% benign lesions. The model uses a CNN integrated with tabular data to output a discrete diagnostic category (malignant vs. benign) rather than a continuous value. Performance is measured using accuracy (95.8%) and ROC AUC (91.6%).

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset from the SIIM-ISIC 2019 challenge, comprising images from the HAM10000 and BCN_20000 datasets. It includes dermoscopy images of skin lesions along with corresponding tabular patient data (age, gender, race, location). The dataset contained over 33,000 images, of which 32,600 were unique after removing duplicates. It also included a test batch with indefinite lesions not present in the training set.

use.training: Used for training the integrated CNN and Tabular model. The data was split into 80% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. The data was split into 10% for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation. The data was split into 10% for testing. The model was evaluated on this split to achieve reported metrics.
use.other: The dataset also included a separate test batch with indefinite lesions not existing in the training dataset, which was part of the original challenge structure.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance in the dataset (approximately 98% benign vs. 2% malignant lesions).
To enhance model training by applying different learning rates to different layer groups of the DenseNet201 architecture.
To measure the overall performance of the classification model.
To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (95.8%) and ROC AUC (91.6%).

SIIM-ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: The model was trained in two stages: 1) Training of fully connected layer group by freezing the model (Epochs: 13, Lr: 3e-6). 2) Training of the model as a whole by unfreezing the model (Epochs: 12, Lr: 1.5e-5, 3e-4). The dataset was split into 80% for training, 10% for testing, and 10% for validation. The dataset contained approximately 98% benign and 2% malignant lesions.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was employed using the DenseNet 201 architecture as the pre-trained backbone. The model was initialized without weights for the CNN component initially, then fine-tuned.
architecture_used.input_size: Images from HAM10000 subset were 600x450; images from BCN_20000 subset were 1024x1024. Specific input size for the model is not explicitly stated, but standard DenseNet inputs are typically 224x224 or 256x256.

architecture_details.integration: Integrated with a Tabular model (containing age, gender, race, location) using a custom 'image_tabular' library written in PyTorch and compatible with FastAI. The integration was achieved via a custom 'CNNTabularModel' function.
architecture_details.layer_groups: The model was split into three layer groups: the first two groups contained the initial layers of DenseNet201, and the last group contained the final fully-connected layers for the concatenated data.
architecture_details.tabular_model_details: Defined with optimal parameters including embedding size, output sizes, layers, and dropout.

training_details.optimizer_strategy: Differential learning rates were applied to the three layer groups.

training_schedule.description: Training of fully connected layer group by freezing the model
Training of the model as a whole by unfreezing the model

training_details.loss_function: Custom weights were passed to the loss function to handle class imbalance (98% benign, 2% malignant).
training_details.hardware: Google Colab with Nvidia Tesla K80 GPU

classification_or_output_head.description: Outputs prediction for malignant vs. benign melanoma cells. The final fully-connected layers were part of the last layer group in the integrated model.

preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate image removal from the dataset to prevent overfitting and confusion
Corresponding record removal from the CSV file for removed duplicate images
CSV file preprocessing using inbuilt FastAI functions
Integration of image data and tabular data into a single 'DataBunch' variable
conclusions: The proposed model, which integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (DenseNet 201) with tabular patient data (age, gender, race, location), achieved an accuracy of approximately 96% (specifically 95.8%) and an ROC AUC of 91.6% in classifying malignant versus benign melanoma cells.
Integrating tabular data with dermoscopy images helps overcome the lack of sufficient parameters and data typically faced when training neural networks on small or imbalanced datasets.
The integration of tabular data aids in improving the model's generalization and global deployment across patients from different regions, races, and genders by accounting for demographic variables.

Transfer learning using the DenseNet 201 architecture is an effective strategy for handling limited datasets, as it reduces training time and mitigates the need for massive amounts of training images.
The model's performance was enhanced by fine-tuning learning layers with appropriate weights and applying differential learning rates to different layer groups.
The study acknowledges that the model's accuracy could be further increased by using a more balanced training dataset and images of improved quality.
The current implementation was limited by the GPU hardware (Nvidia Tesla K80), which restricted the batch size to 32 images, suggesting that larger batch sizes could improve training.
important_remarks: The study relies on the SIIM-ISIC 2019 dataset, which is highly imbalanced with approximately 98% benign and 2% malignant lesions, potentially biasing the model towards the majority class despite the use of custom loss weights.

The dataset contained duplicate images which were removed; however, the paper acknowledges that poor quality images (e.g., those with hair, veins, or improper lighting) hindered training and accuracy.

External validation is lacking; the model was evaluated only on a held-out 10% split of the SIIM-ISIC 2019 dataset, with no mention of testing on independent external cohorts to assess generalizability.
Hardware limitations restricted the batch size to 32 images due to the use of an Nvidia Tesla K80 GPU on Google Colab, which the authors note could have limited training performance.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported accuracy (95.8%) and ROC AUC (91.6%).

The dataset included a test batch with 'indefinite lesions' not present in the training set, but the model's performance on this specific subset is not reported, limiting the assessment of robustness to unseen lesion types.

The integration of tabular data (age, gender, race, location) is claimed to aid global deployment, but the paper does not provide evidence of performance stratification by these demographic variables to verify this claim.
